u4l16.r1: A contest for Paris. The city in question. The nature of the envisioned project. The start date of the project.
u4l16.r2: The association "Bien vivre dans le 2e arrondissement" is launching the contest "Ideas for Paris."
u4l16.r3: It addresses any person with feasible and innovative ideas to improve Parisians' quality of life.
u4l16.r4: Feasible, innovative, or simply brilliant — perhaps it's your ideas that will make tomorrow's Greater Paris!
u4l16.r5: This contest tackles a major problem in Paris: traffic.
u4l16.r6: The proposed ideas should allow finding ways to encourage Parisian and suburban moviegoers to go to cinemas in Paris without using their individual car.
u4l16.r7: All ideas are acceptable, but they will be judged according to the following criteria: originality of the idea, quality of the improvement delivered, feasibility of the solution.
u4l16.r8: Proposals must contain at minimum a presentation of the idea, its advantages, and the conditions for its implementation.
u4l16.r9: Participation in the contest is done via this article's forum, by clicking on "Reply to this article" before December 31, 2009.
u4l16.r10: The jury will meet on January 15, 2010, to choose the three best proposals.
u4l16.r11: Ideas for Paris. Do you have ideas?

u11l44.r1: According to this article, society is going through a great crisis.
u11l44.r2: According to me, it takes real effort to learn a foreign language.
u11l44.r4: The same year, the Larousse médical specified that the shower or bath could be weekly.
u11l44.r5: Since then, the cleanliness habits of the French have progressed: 71% of the French declare taking a shower at least once a day; 7% even say they take at least two.
u11l44.r6: More than one man in four and one woman in ten today declare washing their hair every day or almost every day.
u11l44.r7: Dental hygiene has also improved appreciably: one woman in three and one man in five say they brush their teeth three times a day; these figures seem overstated, however, when one compares them with the sales of toothpaste and toothbrushes.
u11l44.r8: Francoscopie 2010 © Larousse. The magazine Le Point is asking you to write an article of about 200 words on the model of the one concerning French hygiene.
u11l44.r9: With the help of the table above, write a text on the working hours of the French compared with those of inhabitants of other European countries.
u11l44.r10: Invited to dinner at a friend's house, you are very irritated by one of the people he/she has invited because this person spends their time criticizing the French for their hygiene or the fact that they work less than others.
u11l44.r11: You try to show him/her that he/she is not (always) right.
u11l44.r12: Your friend also gives his/her opinion.
u11l44.r14: It would seem that, contrary to received ideas, the French work much more than one thinks.
u11l44.r15: In this study carried out by Eurostat and covering working hours in some European Union countries, one notes that the French place themselves rather well vis-à-vis their neighbors.
u11l44.r16: In fact, they occupy 6th place out of the 14 countries analyzed, with 41 hours of weekly work.
u11l44.r17: It's the Austrians who work the most and the Danes the least.
u11l44.r18: When comparing the data of these three countries, one notes that France has fewer part-time employees than Austria and even Denmark.
u11l44.r19: But it's very interesting to note that in the Netherlands nearly half of workers are employed part-time.
u11l44.r21: Generally speaking, in every country without exception, women are far more numerous than men in being employed part-time.
u11l44.r22: Is it a way of combining family life and professional life, or is it a deliberate policy of the various European Union countries?
u11l44.r23: What the guest can say to... criticize the French. Frankly, I've noticed that the majority of French people didn't wash their hands before eating, and that hygiene wasn't their strong point.
u11l44.r24: When I was an au pair in a family, the children were only allowed one bath a week.
u11l44.r25: And those 35 hours... no French person works the way they do in my country, in Denmark, and people strike far too much over nothing in France. reinforce one's opinion: I really know what I'm talking about — I know a lot of French people!
u11l44.r26: I've worked a few times in companies and I can say that the rhythm was rather relaxed... nuance one's statements: OK, maybe I had bad luck!
u11l44.r27: It's true that, sometimes, they are different!
u11l44.r28: What you can say to... defend the French: Me, I'd say quite the contrary — the French are impeccable and always smell so good... concede that some remarks are justified: It's true, they strike too often, and for that reason, people no longer take their demands seriously.
u11l44.r29: I find that French social services are truly excellent — certainly one of the best in the world.
u11l44.r30: French companies are very efficient, and the pace is not slower than in other European countries.
u11l44.r31: What you or your friend can say to... back up your arguments: In the family where I babysit, I have to bathe the children every evening. give other counter-examples: I find that in fact these famous 35 hours are applied only by a minority, and most French people work a huge amount.
u11l44.r32: Did you see the statistics that came out in Le Point last week?
u11l44.r33: They showed that the French put in on average 41 hours of weekly work, whereas the Danes worked only 40.2 hours!
u11l44.r34: Part-time workers and weekly working hours in some European Union countries [2008]. à en croire qn / jouir de / conseiller à qn de / partager / quant à / s'améliorer. According to him/her, the French really know how to enjoy life.

u11l44.r37: As for me, I have no opinion.

u4l13.r1: Nature weighs so heavily on New York that the most modern of cities is also the dirtiest. [...] From the end of May, the heat crashes down on the city like an atomic bomb.
u4l13.r3: People greet each other saying: "It's a murder." The trains carry millions of city-dwellers away.
u4l13.r4: It isn't the city they're fleeing; it's Nature.
u4l13.r5: Even in the depths of my apartment, I endure the assaults of a hostile, silent, mysterious nature.
u4l13.r6: I feel I'm camping in the heart of a jungle swarming with insects.
u4l13.r7: There's the moaning of the wind, [...] there are the cockroaches running in my kitchen, the elevators that make me nauseous, the unquenchable thirst that burns me from morning till night.
u4l13.r8: Jean-Paul Sartre, Situations, volume III, Rain on New York.
u4l13.r9: It flows tirelessly between the tall cubes of cement.
u4l13.r10: Strange feeling of remoteness in the taxi whose rapid, monotonous wipers sweep away a ceaselessly regenerating water.
u4l13.r11: Impression of being trapped in this city, and that I could free myself from the blocks surrounding me and run for hours finding nothing but new cement prisons, without hope of a hill, a real tree, or a moved face. [...] Rains of New York.
u4l13.r13: And in the gray mist the skyscrapers rise whitish like the immense tombs of this city inhabited by the dead. [...] Terrible feeling of abandonment.
u4l13.r14: Albert Camus, Travel Journals, The City Represented: New York.
u4l13.r15: Both authors also evoke the feeling of suffocation and the anguish that a person feels in a city he no longer controls and which escapes him.
u4l13.r16: The feelings evoked by the authors are negative.
u4l13.r32: Albert Camus, Travel Journals. Nature weighs so heavily on New York that the most modern of cities is also the dirtiest. [...] From the end of May, the heat crashes down on the city like an atomic bomb.

u4l13.r47: It flows tirelessly" — "a ceaselessly regenerating water" — "Rains of New York.
u4l13.r56: "tall cubes of cement" — "blocks" — "new cement prisons" — "skyscrapers rise whitish like the immense tombs of this city." They suggest an impression of anguish, confinement, solitude, and death.
u4l13.r64: Jean-Paul Sartre, Situations, volume III. In this supermarket, customers must weigh themselves the vegetables and fruit they want to buy.
u4l13.r72: I had the impression of being trapped in this city, and that I could free myself from the blocks surrounding me.

u4l13.r74: I have the impression she's made a mistake again.

u4l13.r84: This city is situated by the sea and is partly built on several islands; the Brussels Commission crowned it environmental champion for 2010, and people talk about this city every year at the Nobel Prize ceremony.
u4l13.r85: Example: It's a city that was founded in 1642 and of which half the current population is French-speaking; it's a city where many cultural and sporting events take place and that festival-goers frequent regularly.
u4l13.r86: (Montreal) It's a city that's located in the Middle East, whose majority of inhabitants are expatriates; it's a city that billionaires adore and where you can admire buildings with avant-garde shapes.
u4l13.r87: (Dubai) It's a city that is situated by the sea and is partly built on several islands; it's a city that the Brussels Commission crowned environmental champion for 2010 and of which people talk every year at the Nobel Prize ceremony.

u4l13.r94: There are 6.6 billion inhabitants in the world and a little more than half live in cities.

u4l15.r2: In May 1968, a great protest movement against the established order takes place in France.
u4l15.r3: It begins first in the universities before spreading to the entire country.
u4l15.r4: Violent confrontations with the police take place.
u4l15.r5: Young people protest against authority, want more freedoms in every domain — moral, sexual... — and they make it known through slogans that became famous, like "Under the paving stones, the beach" and "It is forbidden to forbid."
u4l15.r6: May '68 deeply marked French society by freeing its youth from overly strong constraints.
u4l15.r7: Films evoke this period, such as The Dreamers by Italian director Bernardo Bertolucci, released in 2003 with Eva Green, Louis Garrel, Michael Pitt.
u4l15.r8: In this film, Bertolucci stages a period he lived through, since he was a student in Paris in May 1968.
u4l15.r9: The film presents the very free sexual education of three young students — two boys, one girl — during the events of May '68.
u4l15.r10: Other films by Bertolucci: The Last Emperor, 1987; Little Buddha, 1993; Stealing Beauty, 1996.

u8l30.r1: It's thought that the practice of tipping was born in England.
u8l30.r2: The initial purpose was to encourage the friendliness of waitstaff.
u8l30.r3: This practice arrived later in France.
u8l30.r4: At the very beginning, French customers offered a drink "for drinking," never money.
u8l30.r5: This habit became an obligation, in France, not only for waiters but also for many other professions.
u8l30.r6: "What is always included is the service" — "What one also does if one is pleased with the waiter or waitress is leave a tip." Restaurateurs include the tip in the bill.
u8l30.r9: "Yes, the tip is included by restaurateurs" — this statement is in the passive voice.
u8l30.r10: A brief history of the tip. Widely practiced throughout the world, the tip — or "tip," in English — dates back more than three centuries.
u8l30.r11: The tip finds its origins, apparently, in a restaurant across the Channel whose owner, in the 18th century, set up an original practice.
u8l30.r12: He installed on his counter a jar bearing the inscription "To Insure Promptness."
u8l30.r13: This jar was meant to receive a few coins from customers in a hurry who wished to be served faster.
u8l30.r14: The initials of this expression remained and form the word "tip," now meaning "gratuity" in English.
u8l30.r15: In France, this practice developed later, in the 19th century, to reward café-keepers and restaurateurs for quality service.
u8l30.r16: The word "pourboire" meant, as its name indicates, a drink offered in thanks for a service rendered, or a coin to buy oneself that drink.
u8l30.r17: Over time, this habit diversified and the tip came to be given as a favor granted by a grateful customer to cinema and theater ushers, hairdressers, taxi drivers, hotel staff, and more broadly to many providers of daily or occasional services (home-delivery drivers, repair technicians, tour guides, movers, baggage handlers, couriers, etc.).
u8l30.r19: Particularity: Giving or receiving a tip is not customary and is never practiced.
u8l30.r20: Particularity: Waiters, drivers, gas-station attendants, and even dentists and doctors are customarily given a banknote.
u8l30.r21: Don't leave money on the table or counter; let the person concerned know that you're setting aside some change for them.
u8l30.r22: Particularity: The tip is a practice little widespread; Australians don't expect to receive one.
u8l30.r23: Particularity: Waiters earn wages so low that it's imperative to add 10 to 15% to the amount of the bill.
u8l30.r24: This applies in restaurants but never in pubs, where customers serve themselves directly at the bar.
u8l30.r25: Tips in French restaurants. Pierre-Luc, Quebec (Canada). Reply: Hello. Is the tip included in the bill in French restaurants?
u8l30.r26: Alice, Bruges (Belgium). Reply: Yes, the tip is included by restaurateurs in the bill.
u8l30.r27: But one still leaves a few coins for the waiter.
u8l30.r28: The amount depends on the standing of the place, the service, and the smile.
u8l30.r29: Milou, Marseille (France). Reply: You're confusing tip and service charge.
u8l30.r30: What's always included is the service charge.
u8l30.r31: But what one also does, if one is pleased with the waiter or waitress, is leave a tip. ce qui / ce que / ce dont..., c'est / ce sont... What he likes a lot is science-fiction films.

As for me, I observe that customers haven't changed their habits: the practice of tipping... always... in the restaurant sector. 1. is authorized 2. are redistributed 3. was established, is no longer given 4. is practiced. u8l30.r45: He adds for free one or two chocolates with each coffee he serves, or a glass of watered-down syrup for children.
u8l30.r46: The quality of the service, the atmosphere, the mood of the moment determine the amount of the tip.
u8l30.r47: Tips can double a waiter's salary.

u8l31.r2: His posture of withdrawal characterizes a desire to keep his money for himself, and shows his fear of being dispossessed of it. The third character makes a gesture (hand raised with bills flying off) that reveals his tendency to spend money easily, recalling the expression "to throw money out the window." The fifth character turns out the bottom of his pocket to show it is empty, without a penny.
u8l31.r3: He shrugs his shoulders — a characteristic gesture indicating sorrow at having no money. The first character is seated on a safe, symbol of wealth. The sixth character sets his foot on a garbage can from which bills are spilling out.
u8l31.r4: Putting bills in a trash can means that, for her, money has no value. The second character, the young woman, has tucked bills into her garter belt, revealing her attachment to money.
u8l31.r5: She's quite elegant: she's wearing a black dress and high-heeled boots.
u8l31.r6: Her clothing shows that she's attentive to fashion, which requires regular inflows of money.

u8l31.r33: Already, a new wave of customers arrives.
u8l31.r34: Employees from the neighborhood wolf down their sandwiches before rushing in their turn into the big stores during their lunch break.
u8l31.r35: Meanwhile, posted in front of these temples of consumption, union activists from Force ouvrière (FO) hand out flyers to the hundreds of consumers swarming the sidewalks.
u8l31.r36: They took advantage of this day of heavy footfall to make their protest heard against Sunday working.

u9l35.r1: With your neighbor, compare the posters above.
u9l35.r3: The first poster was designed by OGM DANGERS.
u9l35.r4: It's an association that fights against Genetically Modified Organisms (GMOs) in the fields of agriculture and food.
u9l35.r5: The second poster was produced by eat.better.org and the Greens/EFA.
u9l35.r6: It's an organization created by the Greens group in the European Parliament.
u9l35.r7: "GMOs — I don't want them, not on my plate, not in the fields." and "Who really knows the consequences of GMOs?" The first poster is a stylized representation of a plate and the head of a GMO-consumer who seems to be suffering from food poisoning.
u9l35.r8: The cutlery is planted in multicolored fields.
u9l35.r9: The second poster is a photo: a man is threatened by a banana brandished like a pistol at his temple; he fears his days are numbered.
u9l35.r10: These two posters seek to make people become aware of the danger of GMOs and act by refusing them.
u9l35.r13: GMOs — I don't want them, not on my plate.
u9l35.r14: I don't like winter nor summer.
u9l35.r15: GMOs — I don't want them, not on my plate, not in the fields.
u9l35.r16: At 30, Louise Brown, the first "test-tube baby," aspires to... On July 25, 1978, the birth of Louise Brown, the first "test-tube baby," had made headlines across the world.
u9l35.r17: But the Briton, now married and mother of one child, intends to celebrate her 30th birthday discreetly on Friday.
u9l35.r18: While her birth revolutionized the treatment of infertility, allowing millions of couples around the world to conceive through in vitro fertilization (IVF), the young woman is reluctant to celebrate the event in too ostentatious a way.
u9l35.r19: Louise Joy Brown was born by caesarean section on July 25, 1978, at Oldham Hospital, in northwest England.
u9l35.r21: For nine years, her parents Lesley and John had been trying to have a child.
u9l35.r22: But all their attempts had failed until then.
u9l35.r23: They then heard about the work of two Cambridge University doctors, the physiologist Robert Edwards and the gynecologist Patrick Steptoe, who for nearly ten years had been trying to perfect the in vitro fertilization technique.
u9l35.r24: The two researchers combined in a test tube eggs and sperm from Lesley and John Brown, obtaining an embryo that was reimplanted in the mother's uterus, giving birth to the first "test-tube baby."
u9l35.r25: Louise Brown only learned how she had been conceived at age 4, before starting school.
u9l35.r26: "Dad and Mum showed me the video of my birth and tried to explain it to me," she recalls.
u9l35.r27: Her sister Natalie was also born from IVF, four years later.
u9l35.r28: Natalie Brown then became, in 1999, the first "test-tube baby" to give birth — by natural means — to a child.
u9l35.r29: Louise didn't take long to imitate her sister.
u9l35.r30: After marrying Mullinder, a nightclub bouncer, in 2004, she had Cameron in 2006, without any scientific assistance either.
u9l35.r31: Robert Edwards, one of her two scientific "fathers" — Patrick Steptoe died in 1988 — stayed close to the Brown family, even attending Louise's wedding.
u9l35.r32: They met up a few days ago for a ceremony celebrating 30 years of in vitro fertilization at Bourn Hall Clinic, a medical center in eastern England co-founded by Edwards and Steptoe.
u9l35.r33: "Bob (Edwards) is always very busy, but we love seeing him," smiled Louise Brown.
u9l35.r34: "It's good to have such a close relationship.
u9l35.r35: He's like a grandfather to me." LE POINT, Katherine HADDON, 24/07/2008. The harms you do to others won't be long in coming back on you yourself.
u9l35.r37: This phenomenon has raised ethical and anthropological questions: there now exists the risk that humans may be cloned and that we may be able to access the biological identity of each person.

u12l45.r1: This site offers ideas and testimonies of extreme experiences: skydive, drive a Formula 1, set off on an expedition to the North Pole, etc. Cross the Sahara alone — Live on a desert island — Live with wolves for a week — Walk along all the French coasts on foot.
